content.text: In-memory data stores can serve as high-performance message brokers, and Redis is the most widely deployed example. Its native list, stream, and pub/sub primitives allow an agentic AI back-end to decouple long-running agent runs from the HTTP request cycle, coordinate work across multiple worker processes, and fan out live token events to connected browsers — all without introducing a separate broker service during the early stages of a project.

Redis (Remote Dictionary Server) is an open-source, in-memory key-value store that persists data to disk on demand. Originally conceived as a fast cache, Redis evolved a rich set of data structures — strings, hashes, lists, sets, sorted sets, bitmaps, HyperLogLogs, geospatial indexes, and streams — each addressable by a unique key. Because all reads and writes happen in RAM, latency sits in the sub-millisecond range at the 99th percentile on commodity hardware.
For messaging and queuing, Redis exposes three complementary primitives. Lists support a classic producer/consumer pattern where producers push items to one end with
and consumers block-pop from the other with

. Streams (introduced in Redis 5.0) add a persistent, append-only log with consumer groups, message acknowledgment, and the ability to replay history from any offset. Pub/sub channels deliver messages to all currently subscribed clients in real time, trading durability for ultra-low latency fan-out. Together these three models cover the vast majority of messaging patterns needed in an agentic AI system.

Redis is available as a self-hosted binary, a Docker image, and a fully-managed cloud service through providers such as Redis Cloud, AWS ElastiCache, Azure Cache for Redis, and Google Memorystore. The OSS Redis license (BSD) covers versions up to 7.2; from 7.4 onward Redis Ltd applies the RSAL/SSPL dual licence. The open-source fork Valkey (Linux Foundation) continues under BSD.

An HTTP endpoint that runs an LLM-powered agent synchronously blocks the request thread for seconds to minutes, exhausts connection pool limits, and makes retries on transient failures dangerous. The standard solution is to accept the request immediately, persist the job to a durable queue, and return a run ID to the caller. Workers pick up jobs from the queue, execute the agent logic, and write results back to a store the caller can poll or subscribe to. Redis provides the queue, the result store, and the real-time notification channel in a single dependency — a compelling simplification for teams that have not yet justified a dedicated message broker.
Beyond the decoupling argument, Redis offers operational simplicity that matters at the start of a project. Running

gives a local broker in one command. The same configuration that works in development scales to a Redis Cluster with dozens of shards in production. Client libraries exist for every major language, and the command set is learnable in an afternoon. These properties mean teams can adopt Redis as their first message queue and defer the decision to migrate to RabbitMQ or Kafka until the load or feature requirements actually demand it.
— in-memory architecture delivers queue operation round-trips under 1 ms on a local network, making even high-frequency task dispatch practical.
— lists for simple work queues, streams for durable at-least-once processing with consumer groups, and pub/sub for ephemeral fan-out. One tool covers the full range.
— the same Redis instance handles job queues, session state, embedding caches, rate-limit counters, and distributed locks, reducing the number of infrastructure components.
— commands like LPUSH, SETNX, and Lua scripts execute atomically on the server, enabling safe coordination across workers without external locking services.
— Redis Streams consumer groups provide acknowledgment and pending-entry lists so crashed workers do not silently drop messages.
— Redis Sentinel monitors a primary/replica pair for automatic failover; Redis Cluster shards data across up to 1,000 nodes for horizontal write scaling.
— mature client libraries (redis-py, ioredis, Lettuce, Jedis), high-level task queues (Celery, BullMQ, RQ), and first-class cloud managed services reduce operational burden.

— every key lives in RAM. Large backlogs or multi-kilobyte message payloads drain available memory quickly. Maxmemory eviction policies (allkeys-lru, volatile-lru) can silently discard unprocessed messages.
— messages published on a channel are gone if no subscriber is connected at that instant. This makes pub/sub unsuitable for job delivery; streams or lists must handle that.
— Redis has no exchange/binding model equivalent to AMQP. Routing multiple message types to different consumer queues requires application-level logic or separate keys.

— RDB snapshots risk losing up to maxseconds of writes. AOF (fsync every write) approaches disk-speed latency. Choosing between speed and durability requires explicit configuration; the default is RDB with potential data loss on crash.
— the main event loop processes commands serially. While this simplifies consistency, very large commands (e.g., LRANGE on a 100,000-item list) block all other operations during execution.
— Redis Cluster requires application-aware client libraries that understand hash slots. Cross-slot multi-key operations are rejected unless keys share the same slot via hash tags.
A Redis list is a doubly-linked list of string values. Producers call
to prepend a message; consumers call

to pop from the tail and block if the list is empty. The blocking pop wakes as soon as a producer pushes, making this pattern suitable for agent task dispatch where workers should idle when there is nothing to do. This model is fire-and-forget: if the consumer crashes after popping but before finishing the job, the message is lost. The reliability/performance trade-off is acceptable when tasks are cheap to re-enqueue from an external source of truth.
A Redis Stream is an append-only log identified by a key. Producers call
XADD stream_key * field1 val1 field2 val2
to append entries; Redis assigns each entry a monotonically increasing ID of the form millisecond-sequencenumber (e.g.,
XREAD COUNT n BLOCK ms STREAMS stream_key $
to tail the stream from the current position.
Consumer groups add load-balancing and acknowledgment. A group maintains a Pending Entry List (PEL) of messages delivered to its members but not yet acknowledged. When a worker calls
XREADGROUP GROUP groupname workername COUNT 1 STREAMS key >
means 'deliver the next undelivered message'. After processing, the worker calls
to remove the entry from the PEL. A monitoring process can call
to find entries that have been idle for too long (worker crashed) and re-deliver them with
Pub/sub channels are transient. A subscriber calls
for pattern matching) and blocks, waiting for messages. A publisher calls

and Redis delivers the message synchronously to every active subscriber. No history is retained; messages published before a subscriber joins are permanently lost. This makes pub/sub ideal for real-time token streaming to WebSocket connections, not for durable job queues.

Redis serializes all commands on a single event loop thread, making each command individually atomic. For multi-step atomic sequences — for example, checking a rate-limit counter and incrementing it only if below a threshold — Redis supports Lua scripts via
. The entire Lua script executes without interruption, providing compare-and-swap semantics without distributed locks.

The diagram below shows how an agentic AI back-end uses Redis as its central coordination hub. The HTTP API decouples from agent execution by pushing job references into a List Queue or a Stream. Workers in a pool consume those messages, execute agent logic (LLM calls, tool use, memory retrieval), and acknowledge completion. Token delta events flow through a Pub/Sub channel to a WebSocket gateway that pushes them to browser clients. Session state and embedding caches live in the same Redis instance under separate key prefixes.

attrs.alt: Redis architecture diagram showing list queue, streams, pub/sub, and cache in an agentic AI system

content.text: Key design principles visible in this architecture: the HTTP API path is non-blocking (it returns the run_id after a single LPUSH); workers scale horizontally and consume independently via a consumer group; the WebSocket gateway is stateless — it only subscribes and forwards, needing no local state. Redis is the single coordination point, which simplifies reasoning about the system while keeping deployment straightforward.

Redis is the right choice when the team needs a queue immediately and already runs Redis for caching or sessions. Adding a message queue to an existing Redis instance costs zero additional infrastructure. It also works well for medium-scale systems — up to hundreds of thousands of messages per second per shard — where Kafka's operational overhead is not justified.
when job volume is modest, tasks are retriable from a database, and simplicity matters more than guaranteed delivery.
when at-least-once delivery and acknowledgment are required — agent executions that cost money or mutate external state.
for real-time token streaming to UI clients, presence events, or other ephemeral notifications where missed messages are acceptable.
for run metadata (status, start time, model used), idempotency keys (prevent duplicate enqueue on retry), and short-lived caches with TTL.
as the sole audit log for compliance-grade event retention. Stream data can grow indefinitely in memory; Kafka or an RDBMS is better for terabyte-scale, time-bounded retention.
when message payloads routinely exceed a few kilobytes. Store large artifacts (tool outputs, file contents) in object storage (S3, GCS) and put only the reference URL in the Redis message.
The steps below walk through building a minimal agentic job queue using Redis Streams with Python and the
async client. The same pattern applies to Node.js with ioredis.
Create the stream and consumer group once
Implement the consumer (worker loop with XACK)
Implement the reaper (reclaim stale PEL entries)

pip install redis==5.0.4
docker run -d --name redis-dev -p 6379:6379 redis:7-alpine

# setup.py — run once at application startup
import asyncio
import redis.asyncio as aioredis

STREAM = "agent:runs"
GROUP  = "executors"

async def bootstrap(r: aioredis.Redis) -> None:
    """Create the consumer group if it does not exist."""
    try:
        await r.xgroup_create(STREAM, GROUP, id="0", mkstream=True)
        print("Consumer group created.")
    except aioredis.ResponseError as e:
        if "BUSYGROUP" in str(e):
            print("Consumer group already exists — skipping.")
        else:
            raise

async def main() -> None:
    r = aioredis.from_url("redis://localhost:6379/0", decode_responses=True)
    await bootstrap(r)
    await r.aclose()

asyncio.run(main())

# producer.py — called by the FastAPI POST /runs handler
import asyncio, json, uuid
import redis.asyncio as aioredis

STREAM = "agent:runs"

async def enqueue_run(r: aioredis.Redis, prompt: str, model: str = "claude-3-5-haiku-20241022") -> str:
    run_id = str(uuid.uuid4())
    entry_id = await r.xadd(
        STREAM,
        {
            "run_id": run_id,
            "prompt": prompt,
            "model": model,
        },
        maxlen=10_000,   # cap stream length to limit RAM use
        approximate=True,
    )
    # Store initial status so the caller can poll
    await r.hset(f"run:{run_id}", mapping={"status": "queued", "entry_id": entry_id})
    await r.expire(f"run:{run_id}", 86_400)  # 24-hour TTL
    return run_id

# worker.py — the consumer process
import asyncio, json
import redis.asyncio as aioredis

STREAM  = "agent:runs"
GROUP   = "executors"

async def execute_agent(r: aioredis.Redis, run_id: str, prompt: str, model: str) -> None:
    """Placeholder for the real agent execution logic."""
    await r.hset(f"run:{run_id}", "status", "running")
    # ... call LLM, run tools, stream tokens via PUBLISH ...
    await r.hset(f"run:{run_id}", "status", "done")

async def worker(worker_id: str) -> None:
    r = aioredis.from_url("redis://localhost:6379/0", decode_responses=True)
    print(f"[{worker_id}] ready")
    while True:
        # Block up to 5 s waiting for a new message in the group
        results = await r.xreadgroup(
            GROUP, worker_id,
            {STREAM: ">"},
            count=1,
            block=5_000,
        )
        if not results:
            continue  # timeout — loop again
        for _stream, messages in results:
            for entry_id, fields in messages:
                run_id = fields["run_id"]
                try:
                    await execute_agent(r, run_id, fields["prompt"], fields["model"])
                    await r.xack(STREAM, GROUP, entry_id)
                except Exception as exc:
                    # Leave in PEL; reaper will re-deliver after idle_ms
                    print(f"[{worker_id}] job {run_id} failed: {exc}")

asyncio.run(worker("worker-1"))

# reaper.py — reclaim messages idle > 30 s in the PEL
import asyncio
import redis.asyncio as aioredis

STREAM = "agent:runs"
GROUP  = "executors"
IDLE_MS = 30_000  # 30 seconds

async def reaper() -> None:
    r = aioredis.from_url("redis://localhost:6379/0", decode_responses=True)
    while True:
        pending = await r.xpending_range(STREAM, GROUP, "-", "+", count=50, idle=IDLE_MS)
        for entry in pending:
            entry_id  = entry["message_id"]
            old_owner = entry["consumer"]
            # Re-assign to "reaper" consumer so a free worker can pick it up
            claimed = await r.xclaim(STREAM, GROUP, "reaper", IDLE_MS, [entry_id])
            for cid, fields in claimed:
                print(f"Reclaimed {cid} from {old_owner}, fields={fields}")
        await asyncio.sleep(10)

asyncio.run(reaper())
to queue and consume agent prompts. It is the quickest path to a working queue but offers no delivery guarantee if the worker process restarts between pop and completion.

"""
Simple list-based job queue — no delivery guarantee.
Run two terminals: one as producer, one as consumer.
"""
import asyncio, json, uuid
import redis.asyncio as aioredis

QUEUE = "agent:simple_queue"

# ── Producer ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
async def produce(prompt: str) -> str:
    r = aioredis.from_url("redis://localhost:6379/0")
    run_id = str(uuid.uuid4())
    payload = json.dumps({"run_id": run_id, "prompt": prompt})
    await r.lpush(QUEUE, payload)          # push to head
    queue_len = await r.llen(QUEUE)
    print(f"Enqueued run {run_id[:8]}… | queue depth: {queue_len}")
    await r.aclose()
    return run_id

# ── Consumer ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
async def consume() -> None:
    r = aioredis.from_url("redis://localhost:6379/0")
    print("Worker ready — waiting for jobs…")
    while True:
        result = await r.brpop(QUEUE, timeout=0)   # blocks until item available
        if result is None:
            continue
        _, raw = result
        job = json.loads(raw)
        print(f"Processing run {job['run_id'][:8]}… prompt={job['prompt']!r}")
        # simulate work
        await asyncio.sleep(0.5)
        print(f"Done: {job['run_id'][:8]}")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    import sys
    if sys.argv[1:] == ["produce"]:
        asyncio.run(produce("Summarise the latest earnings report"))
    else:
        asyncio.run(consume())

This example publishes LLM token deltas from a worker to a pub/sub channel. A WebSocket handler subscribes and forwards tokens to the browser client. Pub/sub is fire-and-forget — this is appropriate here because the browser can reconnect and re-fetch the stored result if it misses tokens.

"""
Pub/Sub token streaming.
Worker publishes; WebSocket handler subscribes and forwards.
Requires redis-py 5.x async API.
"""
import asyncio, json
import redis.asyncio as aioredis
from redis.asyncio.client import PubSub

CHANNEL_PATTERN = "run:*"

# ── Publisher (inside the worker after each LLM token) ─────────────────
async def stream_tokens(run_id: str, tokens: list[str]) -> None:
    r = aioredis.from_url("redis://localhost:6379/0")
    channel = f"run:{run_id}"
    for token in tokens:
        msg = json.dumps({"type": "token", "text": token})
        await r.publish(channel, msg)
    # Signal end-of-stream
    await r.publish(channel, json.dumps({"type": "done"}))
    await r.aclose()

# ── Subscriber (WebSocket handler coroutine) ────────────────────────────
async def subscribe_and_forward(run_id: str, ws_send) -> None:
    """ws_send is an async callable that sends text to the browser."""
    r = aioredis.from_url("redis://localhost:6379/0")
    pubsub: PubSub = r.pubsub()
    await pubsub.subscribe(f"run:{run_id}")
    try:
        async for message in pubsub.listen():
            if message["type"] != "message":
                continue
            data = json.loads(message["data"])
            await ws_send(json.dumps(data))
            if data.get("type") == "done":
                break
    finally:
        await pubsub.unsubscribe(f"run:{run_id}")
        await pubsub.aclose()
        await r.aclose()

# ── Demo harness ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
async def demo() -> None:
    run_id = "abc-123"
    tokens = ["The", " sky", " is", " blue", "."]

    received: list[str] = []

    async def mock_ws_send(msg: str) -> None:
        data = json.loads(msg)
        if data["type"] == "token":
            received.append(data["text"])

    # Run publisher and subscriber concurrently
    await asyncio.gather(
        subscribe_and_forward(run_id, mock_ws_send),
        asyncio.sleep(0.1),  # let subscriber connect first
        stream_tokens(run_id, tokens),
    )
    print("Received:", "".join(received))

asyncio.run(demo())

Consider a research assistant product where users submit multi-step research queries via a React frontend. Each query triggers a pipeline: web search, LLM summarisation of retrieved pages, citation extraction, and a final structured answer. This pipeline takes 15–90 seconds depending on the number of sources. The architecture:
FastAPI accepts POST /research, enqueues a job to the research:tasks stream, and returns {run_id, status_url} within 50 ms.
A pool of 8 asyncio worker processes each run one XREADGROUP consumer. Workers call a search API, fetch page content, and run 3–5 LLM calls in sequence.
Progress events (search_done, summarising, done) are published to run:{run_id} and forwarded by a Tornado WebSocket handler to the browser.
Completed results are written to PostgreSQL; Redis stores only the job status hash (run_id → status, result_url) with a 24-hour TTL.
A reaper process runs every 30 seconds to XCLAIM entries idle in the PEL for more than 60 seconds (worker OOM-killed by the Kubernetes pod scheduler).

# Sketch of the FastAPI endpoint and worker in the same file for illustration
from fastapi import FastAPI
from contextlib import asynccontextmanager
import redis.asyncio as aioredis
import uuid, json

app = FastAPI()
redis_client: aioredis.Redis | None = None
STREAM = "research:tasks"
GROUP  = "researchers"

@asynccontextmanager
async def lifespan(app: FastAPI):
    global redis_client
    redis_client = aioredis.from_url("redis://redis:6379/0", decode_responses=True)
    try:
        await redis_client.xgroup_create(STREAM, GROUP, id="0", mkstream=True)
    except aioredis.ResponseError:
        pass  # group already exists
    yield
    await redis_client.aclose()

app.router.lifespan_context = lifespan

@app.post("/research")
async def submit(query: str) -> dict:
    run_id = str(uuid.uuid4())
    await redis_client.xadd(
        STREAM,
        {"run_id": run_id, "query": query},
        maxlen=5_000,
        approximate=True,
    )
    await redis_client.hset(f"run:{run_id}", mapping={"status": "queued"})
    await redis_client.expire(f"run:{run_id}", 86_400)
    return {"run_id": run_id, "status_url": f"/status/{run_id}"}

@app.get("/status/{run_id}")
async def status(run_id: str) -> dict:
    data = await redis_client.hgetall(f"run:{run_id}")
    return data or {"error": "not found"}

At steady-state load of 200 concurrent research sessions, this setup uses approximately 60 MB of Redis RAM (each job entry is under 300 bytes; 200 pending × 300 bytes is trivial). Peak throughput on a single Redis primary with AOF disabled exceeds 100,000 XADD/s — well above the research product's requirements. The bottleneck is the LLM API rate limit, not Redis.
— subscribers that are offline or slow miss messages permanently. Always use lists or streams for jobs, and pub/sub only for notifications where loss is tolerable.
Storing large payloads in Redis messages
— embedding kilobyte-scale tool outputs or base64-encoded files in queue entries exhausts RAM and slows XADD. Store artifacts in object storage; put only the URL in Redis.
— failing to acknowledge consumed stream entries causes the PEL to grow indefinitely. Every worker loop must call XACK after successful processing, even in error paths that write failure status.
— streams grow without bound unless XADD is called with MAXLEN. A stream with 10 million unacknowledged entries will exhaust available RAM without warning.

— without a maxmemory limit and an eviction policy, Redis will consume all available system RAM and the OOM killer will terminate it. Set maxmemory to 70–80% of available RAM and maxmemory-policy to noeviction (so writes fail loudly) or allkeys-lru (for pure cache usage).
Using the same Redis DB for queues and cache
— FLUSHDB in tests wipes both. Use separate logical DB indices (DB 0 for queues, DB 1 for cache) or separate key prefixes with a convention enforced in code.
— a standalone Redis instance is a single point of failure. Configure Redis Sentinel for automatic failover in production, or use a managed service with built-in HA.
Keep messages small. Queue only a run_id and minimal metadata; read heavy data from a database or object store inside the worker.
Use streams with consumer groups for any job that costs money or mutates external state. The acknowledgment model prevents both silent drops and double-execution.
Cap stream length with MAXLEN ~ on every XADD. An approximate trim (using the ~ flag) is far cheaper than exact trimming and keeps memory usage bounded.
Set TTLs on all run metadata keys. Hash keys for completed runs that the application no longer reads should expire within 24–48 hours.
Prefix keys by component: queue:, cache:, session:, lock:. This makes debugging with redis-cli SCAN much easier and prevents accidental cross-component interference.
Run a reaper coroutine that calls XPENDING_RANGE with an idle filter every 30–60 seconds and reclaims stale PEL entries using XCLAIM.
Use connection pooling. In asyncio applications, create one aioredis.Redis instance per process (not per request) and reuse it. Each instance manages its own connection pool.
Test with slow consumers. Use asyncio.sleep inside a test worker to simulate a slow consumer and verify the reaper correctly reclaims stale messages.

Redis processes commands serially on its main event loop. A modern single-core Redis instance handles roughly 100,000–500,000 simple commands per second (SET/GET) depending on payload size, network latency, and pipelining. XADD throughput for short entries typically exceeds 200,000 ops/s on a local network. For agentic workloads where the bottleneck is the LLM API (typically 10–500 requests per second), a single Redis shard almost never becomes the throughput ceiling.
When scale demands grow beyond a single primary, the options are:
— replicas can serve XREAD tailing queries for analytics and monitoring, offloading the primary. Workers consuming from consumer groups should always read from the primary.
— a quorum of 3+ Sentinel processes monitors the primary, promotes a replica on failure within seconds, and updates client connection info via a service discovery API.

— shards key space across N primaries using 16,384 hash slots. Each shard handles a subset of keys. XADD throughput scales linearly with shards. Requires hash-tag-aware key naming to keep related keys on the same slot.
— batching multiple commands in a single round-trip reduces network overhead. redis-py's pipeline() context manager groups commands and sends them together.

# Pipelining multiple HSET + EXPIRE calls for bulk status updates
async def bulk_update_status(r: aioredis.Redis, updates: list[tuple[str, str]]) -> None:
    """updates: list of (run_id, status)."""
    async with r.pipeline(transaction=False) as pipe:
        for run_id, status in updates:
            pipe.hset(f"run:{run_id}", "status", status)
            pipe.expire(f"run:{run_id}", 86_400)
        await pipe.execute()

Memory sizing: each Redis hash entry carrying {status, run_id, entry_id} uses approximately 200–300 bytes of Redis memory including pointer overhead. A system with 100,000 active runs uses roughly 25–30 MB for metadata hashes — a rounding error on a 4 GB instance. Stream entries are more compact: a short-field XADD entry with three string fields occupies roughly 100–150 bytes. 1 million unconsumed stream entries consume approximately 130 MB.
(Python) — the official synchronous and async (redis.asyncio) client. Supports pipelining, Lua scripting, pub/sub, and all stream commands.
(Node.js) — feature-complete, cluster-aware async client with built-in support for Redis Sentinel and connection retry strategies.
(Java) — reactive, cluster-aware client built on Netty; the default choice for Spring Boot integrations.
(multi-language) — officially maintained client for the Valkey fork, supporting Python, Node.js, Java, and Go.
— the most widely deployed Python task queue. Uses Redis lists under the hood. Provides retries, schedules, task routing, and a monitoring dashboard (Flower).
— a lighter alternative to Celery. Worker processes call rq.Queue('default', connection=r).enqueue(fn, *args). Ideal for simpler workloads.
(Node.js) — production-grade job queue backed by Redis Streams. Supports retries with exponential backoff, rate-limiting, cron scheduling, and a web UI (Bull Board).
(Python) — middleware-based task queue supporting Redis and RabbitMQ backends. Cleaner message semantics than Celery for greenfield projects.

# Using RQ for simple task queuing — minimal boilerplate
from redis import Redis
from rq import Queue

conn = Redis.from_url("redis://localhost:6379/0")
q = Queue("agent_jobs", connection=conn)

def run_agent(run_id: str, prompt: str) -> dict:
    # actual agent logic here
    return {"run_id": run_id, "result": "..."}

# Enqueue with a 5-minute timeout and 3 retries
job = q.enqueue(
    run_agent,
    "run-abc-123",
    "Summarise the earnings report",
    job_timeout=300,
    retry=3,
)
print(job.id, job.get_status())
— streams every command in real time. Invaluable during development; never use in production (performance impact).
— returns memory usage, connected clients, command stats, replication lag. Scrape with redis_exporter for Prometheus.
(oliver006/redis_exporter) — Prometheus exporter that exposes 200+ metrics including memory, keyspace hits, command latency histograms, and stream consumer group lag.
— Redis Ltd's GUI for browsing keys, running commands, and monitoring streams/consumer groups. Free to use.
By default Redis listens on all interfaces with no password. In any environment reachable from outside a trusted network, enable two layers of protection:
— set a strong password in redis.conf or via CONFIG SET requirepass. Use ACL SETUSER in Redis 6+ to create fine-grained users with command and key pattern restrictions.
— Redis 6+ supports TLS on the main port. Managed cloud services (ElastiCache, Redis Cloud) enforce TLS by default; enable it explicitly on self-hosted deployments.
— place Redis in a private VPC subnet/security group that only the application servers can reach. Never expose port 6379 to the public internet.

# redis.conf — minimum production security settings
requirepass "long-random-passphrase-here"
bind 127.0.0.1 ::1       # only loopback; use actual private IP in k8s
protected-mode yes
maxmemory 3gb
maxmemory-policy noeviction
appendonly yes
appendfsync everysec
— run 3+ Sentinel processes. On primary failure, Sentinels elect a new primary and update client connection info. Failover completes in 10–30 seconds.
— 3 primary shards × 1 replica each = 6 nodes minimum. Automatic resharding on node failure within the cluster. Client must support cluster mode (cluster_mode=True in redis-py).
— AWS ElastiCache (Redis), Google Memorystore, and Azure Cache for Redis handle replication, failover, patching, and backups. They are the lowest-ops path for most teams.
— because Streams deliver at-least-once, workers must check whether a run_id was already processed (e.g., look up run:{run_id} status) before executing.
— after N failed XCLAIM attempts, move entries to a dead-letter stream (agent:runs:dlq) for manual inspection rather than retrying forever.
— liveness: ping the Redis connection and alert if it fails. Readiness: check that consumer group lag (XINFO GROUPS) is below a threshold before routing traffic to a new worker pod.

Redis provides three complementary messaging primitives: list queues (fire-and-forget), streams with consumer groups (at-least-once), and pub/sub (ephemeral fan-out). Match the primitive to the delivery guarantee the use case requires.
Use streams and consumer groups for agent job dispatch. The PEL, XACK, and XCLAIM commands together prevent both silent message loss and duplicate execution when workers crash.
Always cap stream length (XADD MAXLEN ~) and set key TTLs on metadata hashes. Unbounded streams exhaust RAM without warning.
Keep messages small — a run_id plus a handful of metadata fields. Store large tool outputs and file content in object storage; put only the reference URL in Redis.

Pub/sub is fire-and-forget. Never use it to deliver work; use it only for real-time notifications where the receiver can tolerate missed messages (e.g., token streaming to a browser that can reconnect).
Secure Redis in production with requirepass (or ACL users), TLS, and network-level isolation. Never expose port 6379 to the public internet.
Configure Redis Sentinel or use a managed service for automatic failover. A standalone Redis is a single point of failure for the entire job pipeline.

Redis is the practical first choice for a new agentic system that already needs caching; migrate to Kafka only when multi-terabyte retention, strict ordering, or replay by external consumers becomes necessary.
